text: The surgeon prepares to fire the anastomosis, ensuring there is no twist or tension in the tissue alignment.
Upon closer inspection, a small tear is visible in the tissue, though the exact timing of its occurrence is unclear.
The surgeon performs a leak test by driving a nineteen millimeter scope through the anastomosis, observing no bubbles indicating a successful seal.
The surgeon inspects the anastomosis closely, observing no bleeding at the site.
The serosa at the anastomosis site appears to be separating, with visible signs of tearing through the tissue.
The surgeon observes the anastomosis pulling through slightly and decides to reinforce the area by oversewing, a technique previously demonstrated.
The surgeon performs over-sewing on the anterior part of the Baker anastomosis, addressing bleeding observed at the site, potentially due to staples pulling through the tissue.
The video shows the side of the descending colon with a visible staple line, indicating the connection to the end of the rectum.
The surgeon uses a needle driver to work around the right side of the anastomosis, applying traction to achieve a clear view of the posterior aspect of the surgical site.
The rectal staple line is visible on the posterior aspect of the anastomosis, with the EEA stapler having been brought out anteriorly.

The surgeon observes the serosa pulling apart at the lateral location of the anastomosis. They proceed to suture the rectal staple line to the descending colon at the posterior aspect of the anastomosis, ensuring the tissue is pulled up and secured. This part of the anastomosis appears intact with no visible separation.
The surgeon nears the end of the suture line and begins running the suture back to ensure all barbs lock securely in place along the anastomosis.

The surgeon approaches from the opposite side to join the posterior suture line, completing the 360-degree anastomosis between the descending colon and rectum. The left lateral aspect is visibly secured with sutures.

The surgeon runs a suture over the top of the anterior part of the anastomosis, initially using a backhanded and left-handed technique. As the suture progresses towards the middle and right side of the anastomosis, the surgeon switches to regular suturing, demonstrating comfort with the technique despite its appearance.
